items.why: A famous faux ami. German 'Gift' = poison; English 'gift' = das Geschenk. The same Germanic root diverged - in English it stayed as 'something given', in German it became specifically a poisonous gift.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to Berlin' (by train) in German?
items.why: Students use gehen for any 'going'. In German, gehen = go ON FOOT only. FAHREN = go by vehicle (car, train, bus). FLIEGEN = go by plane. To Berlin by train: ich fahre. Saying 'ich gehe nach Berlin' suggests walking there!
Students assume gender follows meaning. German gender is grammatical, not biological. The diminutive suffix -CHEN makes any noun neuter, regardless of natural gender. So das Mädchen, das Mädel.
It's Äpfel. “Apfels” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's beer. “bread” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the Reinheitsgebot first enacted?
items.why: Exact answer: 1516. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
